items.text: Particles vibrate parallel to direction of propagation (Eg: sound)
Particles vibrate perpendicular to direction of propagation (Eg: light)
Point where amplitude = zero
Point where amplitude = maximum
Solids > Liquids > Gases
Fails when source/observer speed → speed of sound; fails when both move at same velocity in same direction
(i) ∠i = ∠r (ii) Incident ray, reflected ray and normal are coplanar
Ray in denser medium hits rarer medium at angle > critical angle → reflected back completely

topics.label: Refraction at Spherical Surface
topics.note: Object in medium μ₁, image in medium μ₂, R = radius of curvature

items.latex: r_1 + r_2 = A \quad\quad \delta = i + e - A

items.text: Image forms before retina; corrected by concave lens
Image forms behind retina; corrected by convex lens
Every point on a wavefront is a secondary source; envelope of secondary wavelets = new wavefront
Same frequency + constant phase difference. Eg: two slits from single source, laser beam
λ decreases in water → β decreases
Angle between transmission axes of polariser and analyser
Reflected ray is completely plane-polarised; reflected & refracted rays are perpendicular
Source moving away → apparent frequency decreases → wavelength increases (towards red end)
Source approaching → apparent frequency increases → wavelength decreases (towards blue end)
SI unit: Coulomb [AT]. Dimensional formula: [AT]
Repel. Unlike charges attract. Repulsion is sure test of electrification.

topics.label: Coulomb's Law
topics.note: ε₀ = 8.85×10⁻¹² C²/Nm². In medium: F_med = F_air/K (K = dielectric constant)

items.text: N/C or V/m. Dimensional formula: [MLT⁻³A⁻¹]
Do not intersect; open loops; parallel equidistant in uniform field; imaginary

items.latex: E = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{P}{r^3} \quad\text{(r >> a, direction opposite to P)}

items.text: Work done in bringing unit +ve charge from ∞ to a point against electric field. Scalar. Unit: V (J/C)
θ = 0° → U = −MB (minimum)
θ = 180° → U = +MB (maximum)
Constant potential at all points; work done on it = 0
Nearly temperature-independent ρ → used in standard resistors, meter bridge
Negative temperature coefficient (ρ decreases with temperature ↑)

topics.label: Biot-Savart Law
topics.note: μ₀ = permeability of free space. Vector form: dB⃗ = (μ₀/4π) i(dl⃗×r̂)/r²

items.sub: Torque on coil (angle θ with B)
Torque on coil (angle φ with normal)

items.text: Angle between magnetic meridian and geographical meridian. Equator value ≈ 17°
Temperature at which ferromagnetic → paramagnetic transition occurs

topics.label: Oscillation of Magnetic Needle
topics.note: I = moment of inertia = m(l²+b²)/12, M = magnetic moment, B_H = horizontal component of Earth's field

items.text: Induced current opposes the change in magnetic flux that produces it

items.latex: \varepsilon = Bvl \quad\text{(rod length l moving with velocity v ⊥ B)}

items.text: Current & voltage in phase (φ = 0)
Current lags voltage by π/2
Current leads voltage by π/2
Z = R (minimum), current maximum
Low V high I → High V low I
High V low I → Low V high I
To reduce eddy current losses. Works only on AC.

items.latex: E_y = E_0\sin(kz-\omega t),\; B_x = B_0\sin(kz-\omega t)

rows: Nuclear reactions; radioactive nuclei; λ~10⁻¹⁰–10⁻¹⁴ m

items.text: Minimum −ve potential to stop photoelectric current
Independent of intensity; photo current ∝ intensity
Below ν₀: no emission regardless of intensity
Highest: Platinum (5.65 eV); Lowest: Caesium (2.14 eV)
Zero. Electrically neutral. Not deflected by E or B fields.
Valid for H-like atoms: H, He⁺, Li²⁺. NOT applicable to He (2 electrons).

items.latex: A = Z + N \quad\text{(Z = protons, N = neutrons)}

items.text: Same Z, different A. Eg: ¹²C and ¹⁴C
Same A, different Z. Eg: ¹⁴N and ¹⁴C
Same N (neutrons). Eg: ¹³C and ¹⁴N
Same A and Z but different nuclear energy states
Uncharged; high penetrating power; diffracted by crystals; isolated neutron t½ ≈ 1000 s; not deflected by E or B
